mnemonics: Tiny Baskets Cover Everything (Totally Bounded = For any given tiny 'epsilon basket', you can cover the set with a finite number of them.)
keyPoints: A set is totally bounded if, for every ε > 0, it can be covered by a finite number of open balls of radius ε.
Total boundedness is a stronger condition than boundedness in a general metric space, though they are equivalent in Euclidean space.
A set is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded.
commonMistakes: Confusing a totally bounded set with a bounded set. All totally bounded sets are bounded, but not all bounded sets are totally bounded (e.g., the unit ball in an infinite-dimensional space).
Assuming that because a finite ε-net exists for one specific ε, the set is totally bounded. The definition requires a finite ε-net for EVERY ε > 0.
